 {"id":"frankenredis-06pn","title":"Add multi-client concurrent access integration test","description":"# Multi-Client Concurrency Test

## What
An integration test that spawns multiple TCP clients connecting to a running FrankenRedis instance simultaneously, executing interleaved read/write operations, and verifying data consistency.

## Why
The TCP e2e tests use a single client. Real Redis deployments have hundreds of concurrent connections. FrankenRedis uses a single-threaded event loop (like Redis) so this should work, but it has never been tested under concurrent load.

## Implementation
1. Add to crates/fr-server/tests/concurrent_e2e.rs
2. Start frankenredis on random port (release build for realistic performance)
3. Spawn 10 threads, each:
   - Opens TCP connection
   - Sends AUTH if needed
   - Runs 100 SET commands with unique keys (client_{thread_id}_key_{i})
   - Runs 100 GET commands verifying values match what was SET
   - Runs INCR on a shared counter key "global_counter"
   - Runs LPUSH on a shared list key "global_list"
4. After all threads join:
   - Verify shared counter = 10 * 100 = 1000
   - Verify global_list length = 10 * 100 = 1000
   - Verify each client key exists with correct value
   - Verify DBSIZE = expected count (1000 unique client keys + 2 shared keys)
5. Test with pipeline depth: repeat above but batch 10 commands per pipeline send

## Logging
- Each thread logs: "[T{id}] SET client_{id}_key_{i} = value_{i} → OK"
- Each thread logs: "[T{id}] GET client_{id}_key_{i} → PASS/FAIL"
- After join: "global_counter = 1000 (expected 1000) → PASS"
- After join: "DBSIZE = 1002 (expected 1002) → PASS"
- Summary: "10 threads, 100 ops each, 0 failures"

 {"id":"frankenredis-07w","title":"Upgrade DUMP/RESTORE to use CRC64 and fix bounds checks","description":"## Problem 1: CRC16 instead of CRC64

fr-store/src/lib.rs line 7580: DUMP payload uses CRC16 (2 bytes). Redis uses CRC64 (8 bytes). This means:
- DUMP payloads from FrankenRedis cannot be loaded by Redis (wrong checksum format/size)
- RESTORE payloads from Redis cannot be loaded by FrankenRedis
- Only 16 bits of protection — trivially forgeable (65536 attempts average)

Note: fr-persist already has a CRC64 implementation (CRC64_REDIS_POLY at line 164) for RDB files. We should reuse it.

## Problem 2: Inconsistent bounds checks

fr-store/src/lib.rs: The String type correctly bounds-checks against payload.len()-11 (excluding trailer). List/Set/Hash/SortedSet check against payload.len() (including trailer). This allows the parser to read trailer bytes as data in crafted payloads.

## Implementation Plan

1. Import/expose the CRC64 function from fr-persist (or implement a shared crc64 utility)
2. Change dump_key to write 8-byte CRC64 instead of 2-byte CRC16
3. Change restore_key to validate 8-byte CRC64
4. Update the payload trailer format: [8-byte TTL][1-byte version][8-byte CRC64] = 17 bytes
5. Fix all bounds checks to exclude the trailer length consistently
6. Update DUMP/RESTORE version byte to indicate the new format

## Interoperability Note

After this change, DUMP/RESTORE payloads will use CRC64 like Redis but our internal payload format (type tags, encoding) still differs from Redis. Full Redis DUMP interop would require matching Redis's exact encoding, which is a separate larger task.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs: dump_key, restore_key, all bounds checks
- crates/fr-persist/src/lib.rs: May need to expose crc64 function as pub","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:09:46.515174128Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T05:29:11.523094869Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T05:29:11.522613116Z","close_reason":"DUMP/RESTORE upgraded from CRC16 to CRC64 (using fr-persist::crc64_redis).  {"id":"frankenredis-5h0q","title":"Report real CPU time in INFO cpu section via /proc/self/stat","description":"# Report real CPU time in INFO cpu section

## What
INFO cpu section reports used_cpu_sys:0.000000 and used_cpu_user:0.000000. On Linux, real values can be read from /proc/self/stat.

## Implementation
Add a helper function to fr-command:
```rust
fn read_cpu_times() -> (f64, f64) {
    // Read /proc/self/stat, parse fields 14 (utime) and 15 (stime)
    // Convert from clock ticks to seconds (divide by sysconf(_SC_CLK_TCK), typically 100)
    // Return (user_seconds, sys_seconds)
    // On parse failure, return (0.0, 0.0)
}
```

Use it in the INFO cpu section.

## Platform considerations
- /proc/self/stat is Linux-only. On other platforms, return 0.0 (fallback).
- The libc crate is already a dependency (used by mio), so sysconf is available.
- Actually, since we forbid unsafe, we should just hardcode CLK_TCK=100 (standard on Linux) or read from /proc/self/status which has a friendlier format.

## Alternative: use std::time::Instant
Track cumulative user-space time via Instant measurements. Less accurate but portable.

## Priority
LOW — cosmetic improvement. Monitoring tools can function without CPU metrics.

 {"id":"frankenredis-5l2","title":"Wire CONFIG SET repl-backlog-size and repl-timeout to replication subsystem","description":"## Problem

Two replication constants are hardcoded:

1. fr-runtime/src/lib.rs line 53: `const DEFAULT_REPL_BACKLOG_SIZE: u64 = 1_048_576` (1 MiB)
   - Used at line 778 to compute the backlog sliding window
   - Redis default is also 1 MiB but allows CONFIG SET repl-backlog-size

2. fr-server/src/main.rs lines 40-41:
   - REPLICA_ACK_INTERVAL_MS = 1000 (1s) — Redis uses repl-ping-replica-period (default 10s)
   - REPLICA_RECONNECT_BACKOFF_MS = 250 — not configurable in Redis either but should be reasonable

3. Replication handshake timeouts (line 993-994): 50ms read / 500ms write
   - Redis uses repl-timeout (default 60 seconds)
   - These aggressive values cause spurious failures on loaded networks

## What Redis Does

- CONFIG SET repl-backlog-size dynamically resizes the replication backlog
- CONFIG SET repl-timeout sets the replication stream timeout
- CONFIG SET repl-ping-replica-period sets the REPLCONF ACK interval

## Implementation Plan

1. Move repl_backlog_size to ServerState (mutable)
2. Handle CONFIG SET for repl-backlog-size, repl-timeout, repl-ping-replica-period
3. In fr-server, read timeouts from runtime instead of consts
4. Update INFO replication section to report actual values

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs`: ServerState fields, CONFIG SET handler
- `crates/fr-server/src/main.rs`: Replace consts with runtime reads
- `crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs`: INFO replication section","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:03.536367224Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:57:28.437166188Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:57:28.436069031Z","close_reason":"repl-backlog-size and repl-timeout wired to ServerState with CONFIG SET/GET support","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-5l2","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-kgn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:16.128244996Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"frankenredis-7ews","title":"Wire LATENCY subcommands to real LatencyTracker data","description":"# Wire LATENCY subcommands to real LatencyTracker data

## What
Replace the stub latency_cmd in fr-command/src/lib.rs (line ~11387) with real implementations that read from Store's LatencyTracker.

## Changes needed
- latency_cmd must accept &Store parameter (change dispatch_argv call site)
- LATENCY LATEST: return store.latency_tracker.latency_latest() formatted as [[event, timestamp, latest_ms, max_ms], ...]
- LATENCY HISTORY event: return store.latency_tracker.latency_history(event) as [[timestamp, duration_ms], ...]
- LATENCY RESET [event ...]: call store.latency_tracker.latency_reset() and return count of reset events
- LATENCY GRAPH event: generate ASCII art from history samples (implementation detail: use Redis's exact format with ± indicators and time axis)
- LATENCY DOCTOR: analyze samples and generate diagnostic text (e.g., "I have no latency reports to analyze" when empty, or "Command latency spikes detected..." when samples exist)

## LATENCY GRAPH format (from Redis)
```
event - min: Xms, max: Yms
+---+
|   |
|  #|
|###|
+---+
HH:MM
```
Each column is one sample. Height proportional to duration. Time labels at bottom.

## LATENCY DOCTOR format
Redis produces multi-paragraph analysis. For initial implementation:
- If no samples: "I have no latency reports to analyze. Be happy!"
- If samples exist: "I have reports for the following events:\n- {event}: {count} samples, max {max}ms\n..."

## Depends on
- frankenredis-a4sf (LatencyTracker infrastructure)

## Testing
- Conformance: update core_server.json LATENCY cases to expect real data after CONFIG SET latency-monitor-threshold + command execution
- Unit test: record_latency_sample then verify latency_latest output format

 {"id":"frankenredis-a4sf","title":"Implement LATENCY sample tracking infrastructure in Store","description":"# Implement LATENCY sample tracking infrastructure

## What
Redis LATENCY monitoring tracks execution time samples per named event type. When a command exceeds the latency-monitor-threshold (configurable via CONFIG SET), a sample is recorded. LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY/RESET/GRAPH/DOCTOR then query these samples.

## Design
Add a LatencyTracker struct to Store (or a new pub field):

```rust
pub struct LatencySample {
    pub timestamp_sec: u64,
    pub duration_ms: u64,
}

pub struct LatencyTracker {
    /// Per-event-type sample ring buffers. Max 160 samples per event (Redis default).
    events: HashMap<String, VecDeque<LatencySample>>,
    /// Threshold in milliseconds. 0 = disabled.
    pub threshold_ms: u64,
}
```

Event types in Redis: "command", "fast-command", "fork", "expire-cycle", "eviction-cycle", "aof-write", etc. For FrankenRedis, start with "command" and "fast-command".

## Implementation plan
1. Add LatencyTracker to Store
2. Add pub fn record_latency_sample(&mut self, event: &str, duration_ms: u64, now_sec: u64)
3. Add pub fn latency_latest(&self) -> Vec<(String, LatencySample)>
4. Add pub fn latency_history(&self, event: &str) -> &[LatencySample]
5. Add pub fn latency_reset(&mut self, events: &[&str]) -> usize
6. Wire CONFIG SET latency-monitor-threshold to update tracker.threshold_ms
7. In fr-runtime's command execution path, after measuring duration, call record_latency_sample if duration > threshold

## Why in Store (not Runtime)
So that both the runtime's command handler AND fr-command's latency_cmd can access the data. The runtime records samples; fr-command reads them for LATENCY LATEST/HISTORY.

## Key details
- Ring buffer: max 160 samples per event (matches Redis)
- LATENCY RESET with no args resets ALL events; with args resets named events
- LATENCY GRAPH: generates ASCII art timeline (see Redis source for format)
- LATENCY DOCTOR: generates human-readable analysis text
- threshold_ms=0 means disabled (no samples collected) — this is the default

## Testing
- Unit test: record samples, verify LATEST returns most recent per event
- Unit test: verify ring buffer overflow (161st sample evicts 1st)
- Conformance: CONFIG SET latency-monitor-threshold 1, run slow commands, verify LATENCY LATEST non-empty

## Files to modify
- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs (LatencyTracker struct + Store field)
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs (latency_cmd reads from store)
- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs (record samples after command execution)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:18:23.929988305Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T02:09:44.201848063Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T02:09:44.201583282Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankenredis-ajk","title":"Implement real XINFO CONSUMERS pending count and idle/inactive time","description":"## Problem

fr-store/src/lib.rs line 5796: xinfo_consumers returns Vec<Vec<u8>> (just consumer names).
fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 4169-4179: stream_consumer_info_to_frame hardcodes pending=0, idle=0, inactive=0.

## What Redis Returns

XINFO CONSUMERS key group returns per-consumer:
- name: consumer name
- pending: number of PEL entries owned by this consumer
- idle: milliseconds since last interaction (XREADGROUP/XCLAIM/XACK)
- inactive: milliseconds since last successful XREADGROUP (Redis 7.2+)

## Implementation Plan

1. Change Store::xinfo_consumers to return Vec<(Vec<u8>, usize, u64, u64)> — (name, pending_count, last_interaction_ms, last_read_ms)
2. Compute pending_count by scanning the PEL and counting entries where consumer matches
3. Track last_interaction_ms per consumer in StreamGroupState (update on XREADGROUP, XCLAIM, XACK)
4. Track last_read_ms per consumer (update only on XREADGROUP with >)
5. Update stream_consumer_info_to_frame to use real values

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs: StreamGroupState consumer tracking, xinfo_consumers
- crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs: stream_consumer_info_to_frame","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:09:21.561101730Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:54:44.406972571Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:54:44.406145205Z","close_reason":"XINFO CONSUMERS now returns real pending count and idle time computed from PEL, not hardcoded zeros","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-ajk","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-7o5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:09:31.307893656Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"frankenredis-as1","title":"Wire CONFIG SET maxclients to fr-server connection acceptance","description":"## Problem

fr-server/src/main.rs line 33 defines `const MAX_CLIENTS: usize = 10_000` and uses it at line 499 to reject new connections. CONFIG SET maxclients updates the config table in fr-runtime but never propagates to fr-server's actual enforcement.

## What Redis Does

Redis's CONFIG SET maxclients immediately changes the rlimit and connection acceptance threshold. New connections beyond the limit get an error response and are closed.

## Implementation Plan

1. Add a `max_clients` field to `ServerState` in fr-runtime (initialized to 10000)
2. In `handle_config_set`, when maxclients is set, update `self.server.max_clients`
3. In fr-server's main loop, read `runtime.server.max_clients` (or expose via a getter) instead of the const
4. Also update `store.server_maxclients` so INFO reports the correct value
5. Remove the `MAX_CLIENTS` const from fr-server

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs`: Add field to ServerState, handle in config_set
- `crates/fr-server/src/main.rs`: Replace const with runtime read
- `crates/fr-store/src/lib.rs`: Already has server_maxclients field (added in f8c5a8a)

## Testing

- Unit test: CONFIG SET maxclients 100 → CONFIG GET maxclients returns 100
- Unit test: INFO clients section reports the new maxclients value

 {"id":"frankenredis-b02","title":"Fix Lua string.format missing argument and pattern capture sentinel","description":"## Problem 1: string.format silent nil substitution

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 3850: When string.format has more format specifiers than arguments, args.get(arg_idx) returns None which becomes LuaValue::Nil. Real Lua 5.1 raises \"bad argument #N to 'format' (string expected, got no value)\".

Fix: Check if arg_idx >= args.len() before accessing, and return an error.

## Problem 2: LuaCapture::Substring(start, 0) sentinel ambiguity

fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs line 3527: LuaCapture::Substring(start, 0) is used as a sentinel for \"unclosed capture\" (end not yet determined). But 0 is a legitimate end position for an empty capture at the start of the string (e.g., pattern \"()\" at position 0). This causes incorrect pattern matching behavior for these edge cases.

Fix: Use a dedicated sentinel value. Options:
- Use Option<usize> for the end: Substring(usize, Option<usize>) where None = unclosed
- Use usize::MAX as sentinel (captures can't be that long)
- Add a separate variant: OpenCapture(usize) vs Substring(usize, usize)

Recommendation: Option<usize> is cleanest and most Rust-idiomatic.

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-command/src/lua_eval.rs: Both fixes in the same file

## Testing

- string.format(\"%s%s\", \"a\") should error, not return \"anil\"
- Pattern \"()\" at start of string should capture position 1 (Lua 1-indexed)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:53.496167480Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:47:37.201427655Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:47:37.200430598Z","close_reason":"string.format errors on missing args instead of silent nil; LuaCapture::Substring uses Option<usize> for end position","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"frankenredis-b02","depends_on_id":"frankenredis-7vn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:57.253625606Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"frankenredis-bfp","title":"Wire CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples to eviction subsystem","description":"## Problem

fr-runtime/src/lib.rs lines 961-962:
- maxmemory_eviction_sample_limit: 16 (Redis default is 5, called maxmemory-samples)
- maxmemory_eviction_max_cycles: 4

These are initialized in ServerState::default but neither is wired to CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples. Our default (16) is also higher than Redis's default (5), which affects eviction behavior fidelity.

## What Redis Does

CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples <count> changes the number of keys sampled during each eviction cycle. Higher values give better eviction accuracy at the cost of more CPU.

## Implementation Plan

1. Change default from 16 to 5 (match Redis)
2. Handle CONFIG SET maxmemory-samples → update maxmemory_eviction_sample_limit
3. CONFIG GET maxmemory-samples → return current value

7. Human-readable summary to stdout

- Run against both frankenredis and legacy redis on same machine

 {"id":"frankenredis-egag","title":"Track real XINFO STREAM radix-tree and deletion metadata","description":"# Track real XINFO STREAM metrics

## What
XINFO STREAM currently returns placeholder values for:
- radix-tree-keys: hardcoded to 1 (should be count of distinct stream entry IDs)
- radix-tree-nodes: hardcoded to 2 (should reflect internal tree structure)
- max-deleted-entry-id: hardcoded to "0-0" (should track highest deleted ID)

## Design
Redis internally uses a radix tree for stream entries. FrankenRedis uses a BTreeMap, so:
- radix-tree-keys: report the number of entries (same as stream length, since each entry is a unique key)
- radix-tree-nodes: report entries + 1 (approximate; radix tree always has at least one node more than keys)
- max-deleted-entry-id: add a new field to Store's stream metadata tracking the highest ID ever deleted via XDEL or XTRIM

## Implementation
1. Add pub stream_max_deleted_ids: HashMap<Vec<u8>, StreamId> to Store
2. In Store::xdel(): update max_deleted_id for the stream key if the deleted ID is higher
3. In Store::xtrim(): same logic for trimmed entries
4. In XINFO STREAM handler: read stream length for radix-tree-keys, length+1 for radix-tree-nodes, max_deleted_id for max-deleted-entry-id

## Testing
- Unit test: XADD entries, XDEL some, verify XINFO STREAM reports correct max-deleted-entry-id
- Conformance: add XINFO STREAM cases with deletion

 {"id":"frankenredis-jg50","title":"Add expired/evicted key counters to Store","description":"# Add expired_keys and evicted_keys counters to Store

## What
Add four new pub u64 fields to Store:
- stat_expired_keys: total keys removed by expiration (lazy or active)
- stat_evicted_keys: total keys removed by maxmemory eviction
- stat_expired_stale_perc: percentage of expired keys found during sampling (for active-expire efficiency)
- stat_expire_cycle_cpu_milliseconds: cumulative CPU time spent in active-expire cycles

## Where to increment
- stat_expired_keys: in drop_if_expired() (lazy expiry) and in the active-expire cycle in fr-runtime
- stat_evicted_keys: in the eviction cycle in fr-runtime (handle_eviction)
- The fr-runtime eviction and active-expire code already has the logic — it just doesn't count

## Key implementation points
- Add fields to Store struct (fr-store/src/lib.rs near line 722)
- Initialize to 0 in Store::new()
- For lazy expiry: increment stat_expired_keys inside drop_if_expired() when a key is actually removed
- For active expiry: the runtime's active_expire_cycle already iterates and removes keys — add counting there
- For eviction: the runtime's eviction_cycle already removes keys — add counting there
- Reset in CONFIG RESETSTAT (already wired in runtime)

## Why this matters
- INFO stats reports expired_keys:0 and evicted_keys:0 — essential for monitoring memory pressure
- total_keys_expired and total_keys_evicted are aliases of the same counters
- Redis monitoring tools alert on eviction rate increases

## Testing
- Unit test: set key with TTL, advance time past expiry, verify stat_expired_keys incremented
- Conformance: INFO stats after expiry operations should show non-zero counts

 {"id":"frankenredis-jkk","title":"Fix NOMKSTREAM behavior for existing empty streams","description":"## Problem

fr-command/src/lib.rs lines 4000-4007: XADD with NOMKSTREAM incorrectly returns nil for existing empty streams (created via XGROUP CREATE ... MKSTREAM).

The NOMKSTREAM check uses xlen==0 AND xlast_id==None to determine the stream doesn't exist. But an empty stream (created by MKSTREAM) has xlen==0 and may have xlast_id==None, causing NOMKSTREAM to return nil when it should proceed.

## What Redis Does

NOMKSTREAM only prevents creating a NEW stream key. If the key already exists as a stream (even empty), XADD proceeds normally.

## Fix

Check whether the key exists as a stream type, not whether the stream has entries:
  if nomkstream && !store.key_exists_as_type(key, \"stream\") {
      return Ok(RespFrame::BulkString(None));
  }

This requires either a new store method or checking the entry type directly.

 {"id":"frankenredis-mmg","title":"Implement SHUTDOWN graceful server termination","description":"## Problem

fr-command/src/lib.rs line 10729: SHUTDOWN command returns OK but does not actually shut down the server. The comment says: \"Stub -- in production this would trigger graceful shutdown\".

## What Redis Does

SHUTDOWN [NOSAVE | SAVE] [NOW] [FORCE] [ABORT]:
1. Optionally triggers a final SAVE (RDB snapshot)
2. Flushes AOF if configured
3. Closes all client connections gracefully
4. Exits the process

SHUTDOWN ABORT cancels a pending shutdown.
SHUTDOWN NOSAVE skips the final snapshot.

## Implementation Plan

Since SHUTDOWN is a runtime-special command, it needs to signal the server event loop to begin graceful termination:

1. Add a shutdown_requested flag to ServerState
2. SHUTDOWN handler sets the flag (and optionally triggers SAVE)
3. fr-server main loop checks the flag each tick
4. On shutdown: flush writes, close connections, drop listener, exit

## Files to Modify

- crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs: ServerState flag, SHUTDOWN handler logic
- crates/fr-server/src/main.rs: Check flag in main loop, graceful exit","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:45.912067485Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:45:05.661284398Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:45:05.660779813Z","close_reason":"SHUTDOWN: sets shutdown_requested flag, server event loop exits gracefully with optional final SAVE","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankenredis-yys0","title":"Precompute ACL category->command mapping in OnceLock","description":"# Precompute ACL Category→Command Mapping in OnceLock

## What

command_categories() in crates/fr-runtime/src/lib.rs:62 and
commands_in_acl_category() in crates/fr-command/src/lib.rs:8924 recompute
ACL category→command mappings from scratch on every call. Each call iterates
21 ACL categories × the entire COMMAND_TABLE (~200 commands) and does
split_whitespace().collect::<Vec<&str>>() on each command's flags string —
~4,200 Vec allocations per invocation.

After the April 9 short-circuit (frankenredis-zjii), the DEFAULT user (no
category rules) never hits this code path. But any user with ACL category
rules (e.g., +@read -@dangerous) still pays the full cost on every command.

## Why This Matters

Production Redis deployments commonly use ACL categories for least-privilege
security. A web app might configure: user webuser +@read +@write -@dangerous.
That user has non-empty allowed_categories, so the short-circuit doesn't apply,
and every command pays ~4,200 allocations for category resolution.

## Design

Use OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> to build the mapping
once at startup:

```rust
static ACL_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();

fn commands_in_acl_category_cached(category: &str) -> &'static [&'static str] {
    let map = ACL_CATEGORY_MAP.get_or_init(|| {
        let mut m = HashMap::new();
        for &cat in ACL_CATEGORIES {
            m.insert(cat, commands_in_acl_category_uncached(cat));
        }
        m
    });
    map.get(category).map(|v| v.as_slice()).unwrap_or(&[])
}
```

Also precompute the reverse mapping (command→categories) so
command_categories() is a single HashMap lookup:

```rust
static COMMAND_CATEGORY_MAP: OnceLock<HashMap<&'static str, Vec<&'static str>>> = OnceLock::new();
```

## Acceptance Criteria

1. commands_in_acl_category uses OnceLock-cached mapping
2. command_categories uses OnceLock-cached reverse mapping
3. Zero per-command allocations for ACL category resolution
4. All ACL conformance tests pass
5. Users with non-trivial ACL rules see no throughput regression vs default user
6.
